questions.question: What is a relapse prevention plan compared to in this lesson?

options.text: A recipe book
A fire escape plan for your mind
A medical prescription
A fitness program

questions.explanation: The relapse prevention plan is compared to a fire escape plan -- you create it when you are calm so you can use it when you are stressed. You would not wait until a fire starts to figure out where the exits are.

questions.question: What are the three parts of a relapse prevention plan?

options.text: Diagnosis, treatment, and recovery
Early warning signs, go-to toolkit (top 3-5 tools), and support system contacts
Symptoms, medication, and therapy
Before, during, and after the crisis

questions.explanation: The three parts are: your early warning signs (subtle signals anxiety is ramping up), your go-to toolkit (top 3-5 most effective tools), and your support system (safe people who can help).

questions.question: Why is it important to create the plan when you are calm?

options.text: Because you cannot write when anxious
Because clear thinking is needed for planning, and in a crisis you can simply follow the pre-made plan
Because the plan does not work during anxiety
Because you need a therapist present

questions.explanation: Creating the plan when calm allows for clear thinking and good decision-making. When stress hits, you do not need to figure things out -- you can simply follow the plan you already made.

questions.question: What are examples of early warning signs that anxiety is ramping up?

options.text: Feeling happy and energetic
Trouble sleeping, avoiding emails, increased irritability, tight shoulders, and withdrawing from friends
Eating more vegetables and exercising
Reading more books and watching less TV

questions.explanation: Early warning signs include trouble sleeping for 2-3 nights, starting to avoid emails or phone calls, feeling more irritable, shoulders feeling tight or jaw clenching, and withdrawing from friends or canceling plans.

questions.question: Where should you keep your relapse prevention plan?

options.text: In a locked safe at home
Somewhere easily accessible -- a notecard in your wallet or a note on your phone's home screen
Only in your therapist's office
In a binder with all your course materials

questions.explanation: The plan is useless if you cannot find it when you need it. Keep it on a notecard in your wallet or as a note on your phone's home screen -- somewhere easy to access when feeling overwhelmed.

title: Your Personal Relapse Prevention Plan - Quiz
